questions.question: What is Dependency Injection (DI) and why do we need it?
questions.answer:
Dependency Injection is a design pattern where an object receives its dependencies from an external source — the IoC (Inversion of Control) container — rather than creating them itself with new.

Why we need it:

  • Loose coupling — the controller/class doesn't know which concrete implementation it gets, only the interface
  • Testability — you can swap real services with mocks in unit tests without changing any code
  • Lifetime management — the container manages when objects are created and disposed
  • Single Responsibility — classes focus on logic, not on creating their own dependencies

.NET has a built-in DI container configured in Program.cs via builder.Services.Add*() methods. It's one of the core pillars of the ASP.NET Core framework.

projectExample.explanation: In FolioTrack, every service is registered in Program.cs. DashboardController never calls 'new DashboardService()'. The DI container resolves and injects it automatically. One gap we identified: most services are injected as concrete classes, not interfaces — ideally should be IAuthService, not AuthService, for proper mocking.

followUps: What are the three service lifetimes in .NET Core DI?
What is the difference between DI (pattern) and IoC (principle)?
What is captive dependency and why is it dangerous?
Can you inject a Scoped service into a Singleton?

followUpAnswers.What are the three service lifetimes in:
The three lifetimes control how often the DI container creates a new instance of a service:

  • Transient (AddTransient) — a brand new instance is created every time the service is requested from the container. Best for lightweight, stateless services. Never shared between callers.
  • Scoped (AddScoped) — one instance per DI scope. In a web application this equals one instance per HTTP request. Everything within the same request gets the same instance. DbContext should always be Scoped.
  • Singleton (AddSingleton) — one instance for the entire application lifetime. Created on first resolution, shared across all requests and threads. Must be thread-safe. Use for caches, configuration wrappers, stateless shared utilities.
builder.Services.AddTransient<IPricingCalculator, PricingCalculator>();
builder.Services.AddScoped<IAuthService, AuthService>();
builder.Services.AddSingleton<IMemoryCache, MemoryCache>();

Quick rule: Start with Scoped. If the service has no state at all and is expensive to create, consider Singleton. Use Transient only when a new instance per call is specifically required (e.g., a non-thread-safe random number generator).
followUpAnswers.What is the difference between DI (patte:
Inversion of Control (IoC) is a broad software design principle stating that the flow of control should be inverted — a class should not control the creation of its own dependencies. The principle says what to do but not how.

Dependency Injection (DI) is one specific implementation technique of the IoC principle. It inverts control by having dependencies pushed into a class (via constructor, property, or method) rather than the class pulling them via new.

Other implementations of IoC include: Service Locator pattern, Event-driven architecture, Template Method pattern.

Analogy: IoC is the principle that says "don't call us, we'll call you." DI is the specific mechanism — the container calls your constructor and passes in what it needs.

IoC (principle)DI (pattern)
What it isDesign principleImplementation technique
ScopeBroad — covers many patternsSpecific — inject dependencies externally
Example"Don't create your dependencies"Constructor injection via IoC container

In .NET, IServiceCollection is the IoC container, and constructor injection is the DI mechanism it uses.
followUpAnswers.What is captive dependency and why is it:
A captive dependency occurs when a longer-lived service (e.g., Singleton) holds a reference to a shorter-lived service (e.g., Scoped). The shorter-lived service gets "captured" and lives for the longer-lived service's entire lifetime — effectively becoming a Singleton against its intended design.

Why it's dangerous:

  • A Scoped DbContext captured by a Singleton will never be disposed — it holds the DB connection open for the entire app lifetime
  • EF Core's change tracker accumulates all entities ever loaded — memory leak and stale data
  • Data from request A leaks into request B (because it's the same DbContext instance)
  • Thread safety breaks — DbContext is not thread-safe
// ❌ Captive dependency — Singleton captures Scoped DbContext
public class ReportCache  // registered as Singleton
{
    private readonly AppDbContext _db;  // Scoped — DANGEROUS!
    public ReportCache(AppDbContext db) => _db = db;
    // _db is now effectively Singleton — never refreshed, never disposed
}

// ✅ Correct — use IServiceScopeFactory
public class ReportCache  // Singleton
{
    private readonly IServiceScopeFactory _scopeFactory;
    public ReportCache(IServiceScopeFactory sf) => _scopeFactory = sf;

    public async Task RefreshAsync()
    {
        using var scope = _scopeFactory.CreateScope();
        var db = scope.ServiceProvider.GetRequiredService<AppDbContext>();
        // db is properly scoped and disposed with the scope
    }
}
followUpAnswers.Can you inject a Scoped service into a S:
No — not directly. The built-in ASP.NET Core DI container will throw an InvalidOperationException at runtime (in Development mode with scope validation enabled) if you try.

Error message: "Cannot consume scoped service 'MyService' from singleton 'MySingleton'"

The safe alternative is to use IServiceScopeFactory to create a scope on-demand inside the Singleton, resolve the Scoped service within that scope, and dispose the scope when done:

public class MySingleton
{
    private readonly IServiceScopeFactory _scopeFactory;
    public MySingleton(IServiceScopeFactory sf) => _scopeFactory = sf;

    public async Task DoWorkAsync()
    {
        using var scope = _scopeFactory.CreateScope();
        var scoped = scope.ServiceProvider
            .GetRequiredService<IMyScopedService>();
        await scoped.ExecuteAsync();
        // scope disposed here — scoped service properly cleaned up
    }
}

Enabling scope validation in Production (recommended):

// Detect captive dependencies in all environments
builder.Host.UseDefaultServiceProvider(options =>
{
    options.ValidateScopes = true;
    options.ValidateOnBuild = true;  // catches missing registrations at startup
});

ValidateOnBuild = true is particularly valuable — it validates the entire service graph at app startup and fails fast rather than at runtime during a request.

questions.tip: Always inject the interface, not the concrete class (e.g., IAuthService, not AuthService). This enables mocking in unit tests. Mention the captive dependency anti-pattern — it shows you've faced real-world DI issues.

questions.question: Explain the three service lifetimes in .NET Core: Transient, Scoped, Singleton.
questions.answer:
• Transient (AddTransient): A new instance every time the service is requested. Best for lightweight, stateless services. No shared state between requests or calls.
  • Scoped (AddScoped): One instance per HTTP request (per DI scope). Same instance is shared across everything in one request. Perfect for DbContext, repositories, and unit-of-work patterns.
  • Singleton (AddSingleton): One instance for the entire application lifetime. Shared across all requests and threads. Use for caches, configuration, and stateless shared state. Must be thread-safe.

projectExample.explanation: FolioTrack uses Scoped for most services — one instance per HTTP request is natural for web APIs. FundHoldingsService is Singleton because it caches expensive API data across all requests. In PaymentReminder (a Worker Service), there's no concept of an HTTP request scope, so everything is Singleton.

followUps: Why is Singleton risky for SqlConnection or DbContext?
What happens if you inject a Scoped service into a Singleton?
When would you use Transient for a repository?
How does Worker Service lifetime differ from Web API?

followUpAnswers.Why is Singleton risky for SqlConnection:
Both SqlConnection and DbContext carry stateful, non-thread-safe context that must be scoped to a single unit of work (a single request or operation). Making them Singleton causes several serious problems:

DbContext problems as Singleton:

  • Change tracker pollution — EF Core's change tracker accumulates every entity loaded across all requests. Memory usage grows unboundedly and queries slow down as EF inspects thousands of tracked entities.
  • Stale data — the DbContext caches entity data. Requests that happen after another request modified the DB see old data because the same DbContext isn't re-querying.
  • Thread safety violation — DbContext is explicitly documented as not thread-safe. Concurrent requests sharing one DbContext cause data corruption or exceptions.
  • Connection held permanently — the underlying DB connection is held open for the application lifetime, consuming a connection pool slot.

SqlConnection problems as Singleton:

  • A SqlConnection can only execute one command at a time — concurrent requests block each other
  • If a connection error occurs mid-request, the Singleton connection is broken for all future requests
  • Connection pooling is designed to work with short-lived connections — Singleton defeats this
// ❌ Never do this
builder.Services.AddSingleton<AppDbContext>();

// ✅ Always Scoped
builder.Services.AddDbContext<AppDbContext>(options =>
    options.UseSqlServer(connectionString));
// AddDbContext registers as Scoped by default
followUpAnswers.What happens if you inject a Scoped serv:
The built-in .NET DI container detects this and throws an InvalidOperationException during scope validation:

"Cannot consume scoped service 'IMyRepository' from singleton 'MyCache'"

This validation happens at request time in Development (or at startup if ValidateOnBuild = true). In Production without scope validation enabled, no exception is thrown — the Scoped service silently becomes a Singleton, which is worse because the bug is hidden.

What actually happens without the exception:

  1. The first HTTP request creates a Scoped service (e.g., AppDbContext) and injects it into the Singleton
  2. The Singleton holds a reference to this specific instance forever
  3. All subsequent requests use the same AppDbContext instance
  4. EF Core change tracker grows without bound
  5. Data from request A is visible to request B (state leak)
  6. Concurrent requests corrupt the DbContext's internal state

Always enable validation:

builder.Host.UseDefaultServiceProvider(o =>
{
    o.ValidateScopes = true;    // catch captive dependencies
    o.ValidateOnBuild = true;   // catch at startup, not at runtime
});
followUpAnswers.When would you use Transient for a repos:
Transient repositories are rarely the right choice — Scoped is almost always better for repositories because they typically share a DbContext, and you want the same context within a request to enable EF Core's change tracking and unit-of-work pattern.

Cases where Transient makes sense for a repository-like object:

  • The repository is stateless and doesn't use DbContext (e.g., a file-based or HTTP-based repository)
  • You explicitly want no shared state between callers in the same request (e.g., a report generator that must see fresh data)
  • The service is very cheap to create and you need isolation guarantees
// Reasonable Transient — stateless, HTTP-based, no shared state needed
builder.Services.AddTransient<ICurrencyRateProvider, FxApiProvider>();

// Bad Transient — two repositories in the same request won't share DbContext
// EF Core can't track entities across them
builder.Services.AddTransient<IInvoiceRepository, EfInvoiceRepository>();
builder.Services.AddTransient<IPaymentRepository, EfPaymentRepository>();
// These get DIFFERENT DbContext instances — transactions don't span them!

Rule: If your repository wraps DbContext, register it as Scoped. This ensures one DbContext per request and correct EF Core change tracking. If it's a pure HTTP/file wrapper with no shared state, Transient is fine.
followUpAnswers.How does Worker Service lifetime differ:
The fundamental difference is that Worker Services have no HTTP request scope. There's no concept of "per request" because there are no incoming HTTP requests — the Worker Service runs on its own schedule.

AspectWeb APIWorker Service
Scope triggerHTTP request starts → scope created; request ends → scope disposedNo automatic scope — must create manually via IServiceScopeFactory
Scoped servicesNatural fit — one DbContext per requestCannot inject directly into the hosted service — must use scope factory
BackgroundService registrationN/A (not a background service)Registered as Singleton automatically via AddHostedService
DbContext usageInjected directly via constructor (Scoped)Resolved inside a manually created scope per execution cycle
// Worker Service — manually create a scope for each work cycle
protected override async Task ExecuteAsync(CancellationToken ct)
{
    while (!ct.IsCancellationRequested)
    {
        using var scope = _scopeFactory.CreateScope();
        var repo = scope.ServiceProvider
            .GetRequiredService<IInvoiceRepository>();
        await repo.ProcessPendingAsync();
        // scope disposed here — DbContext flushed and connection returned
        await Task.Delay(TimeSpan.FromMinutes(5), ct);
    }
}

Creating a new scope per iteration gives you a fresh DbContext each time — preventing stale change tracker state accumulating across execution cycles.

questions.tip: Rule of thumb: DbContext → Scoped, Repository → Scoped, HttpClient → Singleton via IHttpClientFactory, Stateless utility → Transient or Singleton, In-memory cache → Singleton. Worker Services use all Singletons because there's no HTTP request scope.

questions.question: What is captive dependency? How do you safely inject a Scoped service into a Singleton?
questions.answer:
Captive dependency is when a longer-lived service (Singleton) captures a shorter-lived service (Scoped). The Scoped service gets created once and held for the Singleton's entire lifetime, effectively becoming a Singleton — breaking the Scoped contract and potentially causing stale data or shared state between requests.

ASP.NET Core's built-in DI container throws a runtime exception by default if you try this (in Development mode).

Safe solution: Use IServiceScopeFactory to manually create a scope inside the Singleton and resolve the Scoped service within it.

projectExample.explanation: BackgroundService is registered as Singleton. IReminderRepository uses DbContext which must be Scoped. We inject IServiceScopeFactory and create a new scope on each iteration — this gives a fresh DbContext per execution cycle, preventing stale EF Core change tracker state.

followUps: What error do you get when captive dependency is detected?
Can you disable the captive dependency check? Should you?
How does this pattern relate to the Unit of Work pattern?

followUpAnswers.What error do you get when captive depen:
The built-in .NET DI container throws an InvalidOperationException with a clear, descriptive message when it detects a captive dependency. This happens at request time (when the Singleton is first resolved) or at startup if ValidateOnBuild = true is configured.

Exact error message format:

// Error:
System.InvalidOperationException:
Cannot consume scoped service 'MyApp.Data.AppDbContext'
from singleton 'MyApp.Services.ReportCacheService'.

When the error is thrown:

  • Development (default) — scope validation is enabled by default via WebApplication.CreateBuilder. The error throws on the first request that resolves the Singleton.
  • Production (default) — scope validation is disabled by default! The bug silently exists and causes the dangerous captive dependency behavior described earlier.

Enable validation in all environments:

// In Program.cs — enable for all environments
builder.Host.UseDefaultServiceProvider(options =>
{
    options.ValidateScopes = true;   // detects captive deps
    options.ValidateOnBuild = true;  // throws at startup, not first request
});

// ValidateOnBuild advantage: you see the error during dotnet run,
// not when a customer triggers it in production

The error is clear enough to diagnose immediately — it tells you exactly which Scoped service is being consumed by which Singleton. Fix: use IServiceScopeFactory in the Singleton, or reconsider the lifetimes.
followUpAnswers.Can you disable the captive dependency c:
Yes, you can disable it — but you almost never should.

How to disable:

// Disable scope validation
builder.Host.UseDefaultServiceProvider(options =>
{
    options.ValidateScopes = false;  // disables captive dependency detection
});

// Or use a third-party container (Autofac, etc.) that has different defaults

Should you disable it? Almost never.

The only legitimate scenario for disabling scope validation is during integration testing when you're deliberately creating unusual lifetime combinations for test isolation. Even then, it's better to fix the test setup than disable validation.

Common bad reasons to disable it:

  • "The error is annoying" — fix the captive dependency instead
  • "It works in production" — it only works because Production has validation off by default, which is the dangerous behavior
  • "I'll fix it later" — captive dependencies cause data leaks between requests in production, a real security issue

Best practice: Enable ValidateOnBuild = true in all environments so the app refuses to start if any captive dependencies exist. Treat it like a compiler error, not a warning.
followUpAnswers.How does this pattern relate to the Unit:
The Unit of Work (UoW) pattern groups multiple database operations into a single transaction that either all succeed or all fail together. In EF Core, DbContext is the Unit of Work — it tracks changes and commits them atomically via SaveChangesAsync().

The connection: The Scoped lifetime of DbContext (one per HTTP request) is what makes UoW work correctly in a web API:

  • All repositories in the same request share the same DbContext (same scope)
  • Each repository adds/updates entities to the shared change tracker
  • One SaveChangesAsync() call commits all changes atomically in a single transaction
// Both repositories share the SAME DbContext (Scoped)
public class OrderService(
    IOrderRepository orderRepo,
    IInventoryRepository inventoryRepo,
    AppDbContext db)  // same instance as the repos use
{
    public async Task PlaceOrderAsync(CreateOrderRequest req)
    {
        // Both operations tracked in the same DbContext
        await orderRepo.AddAsync(new Order(req));
        await inventoryRepo.DecrementStockAsync(req.ProductId, req.Quantity);

        // Single transaction — both or neither
        await db.SaveChangesAsync();
    }
}

IServiceScopeFactory in BackgroundService and UoW: When you create a new scope per execution cycle (_scopeFactory.CreateScope()), you get a fresh UoW boundary. All operations within that scope share one DbContext and commit together. The scope boundary is the unit of work boundary.

questions.tip: Any time you have a BackgroundService that needs DbContext, use IServiceScopeFactory. This comes up in almost every interview for Worker Service / background job experience.

questions.question: What is the difference between IServiceCollection.Add() vs TryAdd()? What about AddScoped vs AddScopedKeyed?
questions.answer:
Add*() always registers the service, even if already registered. If registered twice, both registrations exist and the last one wins for single-service injection, but both appear for IEnumerable<T>.

TryAdd*() registers only if no registration exists for that service type. Safe for extension methods that shouldn't override user registrations.

Keyed services (.NET 8+): AddKeyedScoped<T>(key) lets you register multiple implementations of the same interface with different keys and inject them via [FromKeyedServices(key)].

projectExample.explanation: TryAdd is important when writing library code. Keyed services solve the 'multiple implementations of one interface' problem without needing factory patterns.

followUps: How do you inject multiple implementations of the same interface (without keyed services)?
What is AddEnumerable and when do you use it?

followUpAnswers.How do you inject multiple implementatio:
Before .NET 8 keyed services, the standard approach was to inject IEnumerable<T> — the DI container resolves all registrations of a type as a collection.

// Register multiple implementations of the same interface
builder.Services.AddScoped<INotificationChannel, EmailChannel>();
builder.Services.AddScoped<INotificationChannel, SmsChannel>();
builder.Services.AddScoped<INotificationChannel, PushChannel>();

// Inject all of them as IEnumerable
public class NotificationService(
    IEnumerable<INotificationChannel> channels)
{
    public async Task NotifyAllAsync(string message)
    {
        foreach (var channel in channels)
            await channel.SendAsync(message);
    }
}

// Or use a factory to select by type at runtime
public class ChannelFactory(
    IEnumerable<INotificationChannel> channels)
{
    public INotificationChannel GetChannel(string type) =>
        channels.First(c => c.GetType().Name.StartsWith(type,
            StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase));
}

Alternative — explicit factory registration:

// Register a factory function
builder.Services.AddScoped<Func<string, IEmailService>>(sp => key => key switch
{
    "gmail" => sp.GetRequiredService<GmailService>(),
    "smtp"  => sp.GetRequiredService<SmtpService>(),
    _       => throw new ArgumentException($"Unknown key: {key}")
});

// Inject and use
public class Sender(Func<string, IEmailService> emailFactory)
{
    public Task SendAsync(string via, string msg) =>
        emailFactory(via).SendAsync(msg);
}

With .NET 8 keyed services, the factory pattern is no longer needed — use AddKeyedScoped + [FromKeyedServices] instead. Cleaner and container-native.
followUpAnswers.What is AddEnumerable and when do you us:
TryAddEnumerable (note: it's TryAddEnumerable, not AddEnumerable) is a specialized registration method that adds a service to the collection only if the exact same implementation type isn't already registered for that service type. It prevents duplicate registrations while still allowing multiple different implementations.

// Regular Add: always adds, allows duplicates
services.AddScoped<IValidator, EmailValidator>();
services.AddScoped<IValidator, EmailValidator>();  // duplicate! now registered twice

// TryAdd: only adds if NO registration exists for the service type
services.TryAddScoped<IValidator, EmailValidator>();
services.TryAddScoped<IValidator, EmailValidator>();  // second call is a no-op
// Result: EmailValidator registered once

// TryAddEnumerable: adds only if this specific implementation isn't registered
services.TryAddEnumerable(
    ServiceDescriptor.Scoped<IValidator, EmailValidator>());
services.TryAddEnumerable(
    ServiceDescriptor.Scoped<IValidator, PhoneValidator>());  // added — different type
services.TryAddEnumerable(
    ServiceDescriptor.Scoped<IValidator, EmailValidator>());  // no-op — already registered
// Result: EmailValidator once, PhoneValidator once — no duplicates

Primary use case: Library/framework authors who call AddXyz() extension methods multiple times without wanting to duplicate registrations. ASP.NET Core itself uses TryAddEnumerable extensively internally to register framework services idempotently.

Application code rarely needs TryAddEnumerable — it's mainly for infrastructure and library code that might be called multiple times during setup.

questions.tip: Keyed services (AddKeyedScoped) were added in .NET 8 — a great answer if the interviewer asks about new .NET 8 DI features.

questions.question: How does property injection work in .NET Core? Why is constructor injection preferred?
questions.answer:
The built-in .NET DI container does not support property injection out of the box — it only does constructor injection. Property injection requires third-party containers like Autofac or Castle Windsor.

Constructor injection is preferred because:

  • Dependencies are explicit and required — the object cannot be constructed without them
  • Immutable — set once in the constructor, never changed
  • Easier to unit test — you can see all dependencies from the constructor signature
  • Fails fast — missing dependency = exception at startup, not at runtime

Property injection creates optional dependencies which can lead to null reference exceptions if not set.

projectExample.explanation: Every controller in FolioTrack uses constructor injection. Missing services would cause an exception at startup during DI container validation, not silently fail at runtime.

followUps: What happens if you have too many constructor parameters?
What is the service locator anti-pattern?
How do you use IServiceProvider directly (and when should you avoid it)?

followUpAnswers.What happens if you have too many constr:
Too many constructor parameters (generally more than 4–5) is a code smell — it strongly suggests the class is violating the Single Responsibility Principle (SRP) and doing too many things.

What it indicates:

  • The class has too many concerns — it should be split into smaller, focused classes
  • Logic that belongs in a service is living in a controller or aggregate service
  • Missing domain abstraction — several related dependencies could be grouped into a single cohesive service
// ❌ Constructor over-injection — 7 parameters is too many
public class ReportController(
    IUserRepository userRepo,
    IInvoiceRepository invoiceRepo,
    IPaymentRepository paymentRepo,
    IEmailService emailSvc,
    IPdfService pdfSvc,
    ILogger<ReportController> logger,
    IMapper mapper)
{ }

// ✅ Refactored — extract a cohesive ReportService
public class ReportController(
    IReportService reportSvc,   // encapsulates repos + pdf logic
    ILogger<ReportController> logger)
{ }

public class ReportService(
    IUserRepository userRepo,
    IInvoiceRepository invoiceRepo,
    IPaymentRepository paymentRepo,
    IPdfService pdfSvc)
{ }

Legitimate exceptions: Aggregate services or orchestrators (e.g., a CheckoutService that genuinely coordinates inventory, payment, email, and order repos) may need more dependencies. Use judgment — the controller shouldn't be doing the orchestration.

Mention this in an interview as self-awareness: "More than 4–5 dependencies in a constructor usually means I need to refactor."
followUpAnswers.What is the service locator anti-pattern:
The service locator anti-pattern is when a class resolves its own dependencies at runtime by calling IServiceProvider.GetService<T>() directly, instead of declaring them in the constructor.

Why it's an anti-pattern:

  • Hidden dependencies — a class's dependencies are not visible from its constructor signature; you must read the full method body
  • Breaks testability — you can't inject mocks because the class controls its own resolution; you'd have to set up a full container in tests
  • Violates DIP — the class is coupled to the DI container, not just its abstractions
  • Runtime failures — a missing registration throws at the call site inside the method, not at startup
  • Lies about dependencies — a class with IServiceProvider in its constructor could secretly depend on anything
// ❌ Service locator — hidden dependencies
public class InvoiceProcessor
{
    private readonly IServiceProvider _sp;
    public InvoiceProcessor(IServiceProvider sp) => _sp = sp;

    public void Process()
    {
        var db = _sp.GetRequiredService<AppDbContext>();   // hidden!
        var email = _sp.GetRequiredService<IEmailService>(); // hidden!
    }
}

// ✅ Constructor injection — dependencies explicit
public class InvoiceProcessor(
    AppDbContext db,
    IEmailService emailSvc)
{
    public void Process() { /* uses db and emailSvc */ }
}

The only acceptable use of IServiceProvider directly in application code is in abstract factory implementations where the concrete type is not known until runtime.
followUpAnswers.How do you use IServiceProvider directly:
You inject IServiceProvider directly when you need dynamic, runtime-determined service resolution — where the type to resolve isn't known at compile time.

When it IS acceptable:

  1. Factory pattern — resolving one of several possible implementations based on a runtime value
  2. Singleton needing Scoped services — via IServiceScopeFactory (which is the preferred approach)
  3. Infrastructure/framework code — middleware, custom conventions, generic pipelines
  4. Background service scope creation — though prefer IServiceScopeFactory
// Acceptable — factory pattern, type determined at runtime
public class ExporterFactory(IServiceProvider sp)
{
    public IExporter Create(ExportFormat format) => format switch
    {
        ExportFormat.Excel => sp.GetRequiredService<ExcelExporter>(),
        ExportFormat.Pdf   => sp.GetRequiredService<PdfExporter>(),
        ExportFormat.Csv   => sp.GetRequiredService<CsvExporter>(),
        _ => throw new NotSupportedException()
    };
}

// Acceptable — middleware needing scoped service (singleton middleware)
public class AuditMiddleware(RequestDelegate next, IServiceProvider sp)
{
    public async Task InvokeAsync(HttpContext ctx)
    {
        // Middleware instances are singletons — create scope manually
        using var scope = sp.CreateScope();
        var audit = scope.ServiceProvider.GetRequiredService<IAuditService>();
        await audit.LogAsync(ctx.Request.Path);
        await next(ctx);
    }
}

When to avoid: In any regular service, repository, or controller — always use constructor injection instead. If you find yourself using IServiceProvider in business logic, it's a design smell.

questions.tip: Interviewer tip: If a class has more than 4–5 constructor parameters, it's usually a sign that the class has too many responsibilities — it should be split (SRP). Mention this as self-awareness.

questions.question: What is IOptions<T>, IOptionsSnapshot<T>, and IOptionsMonitor<T>? When do you use each?
questions.answer:
These are .NET's typed configuration options pattern — strongly-typed access to appsettings.json values.

  • IOptions<T>: Singleton. Reads config once at startup. Does not pick up changes to appsettings.json at runtime. Use for config that never changes.
  • IOptionsSnapshot<T>: Scoped. Reads fresh config per request. Supports hot reload. Cannot be injected into Singletons (captive dependency).
  • IOptionsMonitor<T>: Singleton. Watches for config changes in real-time and fires OnChange callback. Use in Singletons that need live config updates.

projectExample.explanation: Both projects use IOptions<T> for JWT and SMTP config — startup configuration that doesn't need runtime updates. IOptionsMonitor would be overkill here. The key benefit: no magic strings for config keys, type safety, easy unit testing with fake options.

followUps: How do you validate IOptions<T> at startup?
What is options validation with DataAnnotations?

followUpAnswers.How do you validate IOptions<T> at start:
By default, IOptions<T> configuration is read lazily on first access — a missing or invalid config value only throws when code tries to use options.Value. Startup validation makes the app refuse to start if configuration is invalid, giving you a clear error immediately.

Two levels of startup validation:

// 1. DataAnnotation validation
builder.Services
    .AddOptions<JwtSettings>()
    .BindConfiguration("JwtSettings")  // binds from appsettings.json
    .ValidateDataAnnotations()         // runs [Required], [Range] etc.
    .ValidateOnStart();                // throws at startup if invalid

// 2. Custom validation logic
builder.Services
    .AddOptions<SmtpSettings>()
    .BindConfiguration("Smtp")
    .Validate(smtp =>
    {
        if (smtp.Port is < 1 or > 65535)
            return false;
        if (string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(smtp.Host))
            return false;
        return true;
    }, "SMTP settings are invalid — check Host and Port")
    .ValidateOnStart();

// 3. Complex validation via IValidateOptions
public class JwtSettingsValidator : IValidateOptions<JwtSettings>
{
    public ValidateOptionsResult Validate(string? name, JwtSettings settings)
    {
        if (settings.Key.Length < 32)
            return ValidateOptionsResult.Fail(
                "JWT key must be at least 32 characters for HS256");
        if (settings.ExpiryDays is < 1 or > 365)
            return ValidateOptionsResult.Fail(
                "JWT expiry must be between 1 and 365 days");
        return ValidateOptionsResult.Success;
    }
}
builder.Services.AddSingleton<IValidateOptions<JwtSettings>, JwtSettingsValidator>();

Result: App fails at startup with OptionsValidationException listing all invalid config values — no silent misconfiguration bugs in production.
followUpAnswers.What is options validation with DataAnno:
When you decorate your options class with data annotation attributes and call .ValidateDataAnnotations(), the options framework runs the same validation that ASP.NET Core uses for model validation — but against your configuration class at startup.

// Options class with DataAnnotation validation
public class JwtSettings
{
    [Required(ErrorMessage = "JWT Key is required")]
    [MinLength(32, ErrorMessage = "JWT Key must be at least 32 characters")]
    public string Key { get; set; } = string.Empty;

    [Required]
    public string Issuer { get; set; } = string.Empty;

    [Required]
    public string Audience { get; set; } = string.Empty;

    [Range(1, 365, ErrorMessage = "Expiry must be 1-365 days")]
    public int ExpiryDays { get; set; } = 7;
}

public class SmtpSettings
{
    [Required]
    public string Host { get; set; } = string.Empty;

    [Range(1, 65535)]
    public int Port { get; set; } = 587;

    [Required]
    [EmailAddress]
    public string Username { get; set; } = string.Empty;
}

// Registration — binds, validates at startup
builder.Services.AddOptions<JwtSettings>()
    .BindConfiguration("JwtSettings")
    .ValidateDataAnnotations()
    .ValidateOnStart();

builder.Services.AddOptions<SmtpSettings>()
    .BindConfiguration("Smtp")
    .ValidateDataAnnotations()
    .ValidateOnStart();

What happens if validation fails:

// App throws on startup:
Microsoft.Extensions.Options.OptionsValidationException:
  DataAnnotation validation failed for 'JwtSettings' with 2 error(s):
  - Key: JWT Key is required
  - ExpiryDays: Expiry must be 1-365 days

This is far better than a NullReferenceException deep in a JWT token generation method at 2am when the first authenticated request comes in.

questions.tip: Mention builder.Services.AddOptions().ValidateDataAnnotations().ValidateOnStart() for startup validation — it catches missing config immediately instead of at runtime.

questions.question: How do you register open generic types in .NET DI? Give a real example.
questions.answer:
Open generic registration lets you register a generic service without specifying the type parameter — the DI container fills it in at resolution time.

Syntax: services.AddScoped(typeof(IRepository<>), typeof(GenericRepository<>))

When you inject IRepository<Product>, the container automatically creates a GenericRepository<Product>. This is the basis of the generic repository pattern and eliminates boilerplate per-entity repository registrations.

projectExample.explanation: One registration handles all entity types. InvestmentController can inject IRepository<Investment>, UserController can inject IRepository<User> — both resolved automatically without separate registrations.

followUps: How do you override the generic registration for a specific type?
Can you mix open generic and closed generic registrations?

followUpAnswers.How do you override the generic registra:
Register a specific closed generic after the open generic. The DI container prefers the most-specific (closed generic) registration when resolving.

// Open generic — handles all T by default
builder.Services.AddScoped(
    typeof(IRepository<>),
    typeof(EfCoreRepository<>));

// Override for Order specifically — needs custom logic (caching, audit trail)
builder.Services.AddScoped<
    IRepository<Order>,
    CachedOrderRepository>();

// Verification:
// Injecting IRepository<Product> → gets EfCoreRepository<Product> (open generic)
// Injecting IRepository<User>    → gets EfCoreRepository<User>    (open generic)
// Injecting IRepository<Order>   → gets CachedOrderRepository      (specific override)

Why this works: The DI container searches registrations in reverse order (last registered = highest priority for the same service type). A closed generic registration for IRepository<Order> is a more specific service descriptor than the open IRepository<>, so it wins.

Practical use case: You have a generic EfCoreRepository<T> for 90% of entities, but Order needs a decorated version with caching, audit logging, or specific query optimizations. Override just that one type without touching the rest.
followUpAnswers.Can you mix open generic and closed gene:
Yes — this is the primary value of the pattern. Open and closed generic registrations coexist in the same IServiceCollection and the container selects the most specific match when resolving.

// Open generic (catch-all)
builder.Services.AddScoped(
    typeof(IRepository<>),
    typeof(EfCoreRepository<>));

// Closed generics (specific overrides)
builder.Services.AddScoped<IRepository<Invoice>, InvoiceRepository>();
builder.Services.AddScoped<IRepository<Payment>, PaymentRepository>();

// Resolution:
// IRepository<Product> → EfCoreRepository<Product>  (open generic)
// IRepository<User>    → EfCoreRepository<User>     (open generic)
// IRepository<Invoice> → InvoiceRepository           (closed override)
// IRepository<Payment> → PaymentRepository           (closed override)

Advanced: open generics with constraints

// You can also have different open generics for different constraint hierarchies
// (less common, requires Scrutor or manual registration logic)

// Pipeline/handler patterns use this extensively:
builder.Services.AddScoped(
    typeof(IRequestHandler<,>),
    typeof(LoggingRequestHandler<,>));  // decorator for all handlers

builder.Services.AddScoped(
    typeof(IRequestHandler<,>),
    typeof(ConcreteHandler<,>));        // actual handler

Registration order matters when multiple registrations match the same type. For IEnumerable<T> injection (getting all implementations), all matching registrations are returned in registration order.

questions.tip: This pattern is common in enterprise codebases. If you've used it, say so — it shows you understand advanced DI patterns beyond the basics.

questions.question: What is IHttpClientFactory and why should you always use it instead of new HttpClient()?
questions.answer:
Creating new HttpClient() for each request causes socket exhaustion — each instance holds a socket open even after disposal because of TCP TIME_WAIT. This exhausts available ports under load.

IHttpClientFactory manages a pool of HttpMessageHandler instances, reusing connections while properly rotating them after a configurable period (default 2 minutes). It integrates with .NET's DI and supports Polly for retry/circuit-breaker policies.

projectExample.explanation: CSInvoice originally used 'new HttpClient()' inside loops — this caused socket exhaustion under load. Fixed by switching to IHttpClientFactory with typed clients. FolioTrack uses AddHttpClient<MfApiService>() for the mutual fund API calls — cleaner and properly pooled.

followUps: How do you add Polly retry policy to IHttpClientFactory?
What is the difference between typed, named, and basic clients?
What is handler lifetime and why does it matter?

followUpAnswers.How do you add Polly retry policy to IHt:
Polly is a .NET resilience library that adds retry, circuit breaker, timeout, and bulkhead isolation policies. It integrates directly with IHttpClientFactory via the Microsoft.Extensions.Http.Polly package.

// Install: dotnet add package Microsoft.Extensions.Http.Polly
//          dotnet add package Polly.Extensions.Http

// Retry policy — retry on transient HTTP errors (5xx, 408)
var retryPolicy = HttpPolicyExtensions
    .HandleTransientHttpError()   // handles HttpRequestException, 5xx, 408
    .WaitAndRetryAsync(
        retryCount: 3,
        sleepDurationProvider: attempt =>
            TimeSpan.FromSeconds(Math.Pow(2, attempt)), // exponential: 2s, 4s, 8s
        onRetry: (outcome, delay, attempt, ctx) =>
            Log.Warning("Retry {attempt} after {delay}ms. Reason: {reason}",
                attempt, delay.TotalMilliseconds, outcome.Exception?.Message));

// Circuit breaker — stop trying after 5 failures in 30s
var circuitBreakerPolicy = HttpPolicyExtensions
    .HandleTransientHttpError()
    .CircuitBreakerAsync(
        handledEventsAllowedBeforeBreaking: 5,
        durationOfBreak: TimeSpan.FromSeconds(30));

// Apply to typed client
builder.Services.AddHttpClient<MfApiService>(client =>
{
    client.BaseAddress = new Uri("https://api.mfapi.in/");
    client.Timeout = TimeSpan.FromSeconds(10);
})
.AddPolicyHandler(retryPolicy)
.AddPolicyHandler(circuitBreakerPolicy);

// .NET 8 Microsoft.Extensions.Http.Resilience (newer, built-in)
builder.Services.AddHttpClient<MfApiService>()
    .AddStandardResilienceHandler();  // retry + circuit breaker built in
followUpAnswers.What is the difference between typed, na:
IHttpClientFactory supports three patterns for creating and consuming HttpClient instances, each suited to different scenarios:

TypeRegistrationInjectionBest for
BasicAddHttpClient()IHttpClientFactory, then factory.CreateClient()Dynamic client creation, multiple endpoints
NamedAddHttpClient("name", ...)factory.CreateClient("name")Multiple pre-configured clients in same class
TypedAddHttpClient<TService>()Inject HttpClient via constructor of TServiceDedicated service class per external API
// Basic — least opinionated
services.AddHttpClient();
public class MyService(IHttpClientFactory factory)
{
    var client = factory.CreateClient();  // unconfigured, default settings
}

// Named — pre-configured, referenced by string
services.AddHttpClient("GitHub", c =>
    c.BaseAddress = new Uri("https://api.github.com/"));
public class GitHubService(IHttpClientFactory factory)
{
    var client = factory.CreateClient("GitHub");
}

// Typed — cleanest, most DI-idiomatic
services.AddHttpClient<GitHubService>(c =>
    c.BaseAddress = new Uri("https://api.github.com/"));
public class GitHubService(HttpClient client)  // HttpClient injected directly
{
    public async Task<string> GetUserAsync(string username) =>
        await client.GetStringAsync($"/users/{username}");
}

Prefer typed clients — they encapsulate all HTTP logic for an external service, are testable by mocking HttpClient, and have no magic strings.
followUpAnswers.What is handler lifetime and why does it:
The handler lifetime refers to how long the underlying HttpMessageHandler (which manages the actual TCP connection/socket) lives before being rotated out. This is the core mechanism IHttpClientFactory uses to prevent both socket exhaustion and DNS staleness.

The problem IHttpClientFactory solves:

  • New HttpClient() per request — socket exhaustion: each client holds a socket in TIME_WAIT even after disposal
  • Single static HttpClient — DNS staleness: the cached DNS entry never refreshes, so if the server's IP changes, the client keeps sending to the old IP

IHttpClientFactory's solution:

  • Creates a pool of HttpMessageHandler instances
  • Each HttpClient created by the factory shares a handler from the pool
  • Handlers are rotated every 2 minutes by default — old handlers aren't disposed immediately (active requests complete), but new requests get fresh handlers with fresh DNS lookups
  • Disposed HttpClient instances release their reference to the handler, but the handler itself stays alive until the rotation period expires
// Configure handler lifetime per client
builder.Services.AddHttpClient<MfApiService>()
    .SetHandlerLifetime(TimeSpan.FromMinutes(5));  // rotate every 5 minutes

// Default is 2 minutes — good balance:
// - Short enough to pick up DNS changes
// - Long enough to amortize connection setup cost

Rule: Never dispose HttpClient instances created by the factory in a using block — disposing the client doesn't close the socket (the handler manages that), and you'd be creating/returning handlers unnecessarily.

questions.tip: Mentioning CSInvoice's socket exhaustion bug and fix is a great real-world story for this question. Always use IHttpClientFactory or AddHttpClient<T>() — never new HttpClient() inside a class.

questions.question: What is the service locator pattern? Why is it considered an anti-pattern?
questions.answer:
The service locator pattern is when a class calls IServiceProvider.GetService<T>() directly to resolve its dependencies instead of declaring them in the constructor.

Why it's an anti-pattern:

  • Dependencies are hidden — you can't tell what a class needs without reading the code
  • Testability breaks — you can't easily mock because the class controls its own resolution
  • Runtime failures — missing registration causes exception at the call site, not at startup
  • Violates DIP — the class is coupled to the container, not just the abstraction

When it IS acceptable: In factory classes, background services, or middleware that genuinely need dynamic resolution at runtime (and that's the only use).

projectExample.explanation: Constructor injection makes dependencies explicit. If ReportService needs AppDbContext, that's immediately visible from the constructor. With service locator, a reviewer or tester has to read the entire method body to discover what the class actually needs.

followUps: When is using IServiceProvider directly acceptable?
What is the difference between service locator and factory pattern?

followUpAnswers.When is using IServiceProvider directly:
While the service locator pattern is generally an anti-pattern in application code, there are specific infrastructure and framework-level scenarios where IServiceProvider is the right tool:

1. Abstract factory when type is runtime-determined:

// Type isn't known at compile time — must resolve dynamically
public class HandlerFactory(IServiceProvider sp)
{
    public ICommandHandler GetHandler(CommandType type) => type switch
    {
        CommandType.Create => sp.GetRequiredService<CreateCommandHandler>(),
        CommandType.Delete => sp.GetRequiredService<DeleteCommandHandler>(),
        _ => throw new NotSupportedException()
    };
}

2. Middleware (Singleton context needing Scoped services):

// Middleware is Singleton — must create scope manually
public class RequestLoggingMiddleware(RequestDelegate next, IServiceProvider sp)
{
    public async Task InvokeAsync(HttpContext ctx)
    {
        using var scope = sp.CreateScope();
        var logger = scope.ServiceProvider.GetRequiredService<IAuditLogger>();
        await logger.LogRequestAsync(ctx);
        await next(ctx);
    }
}

3. BackgroundService with Scoped dependencies: Already covered — use IServiceScopeFactory (which is itself injected via constructor, so it's technically still constructor injection).

4. Extension method / library setup code:

// Acceptable in startup/initialization code
var sp = builder.Services.BuildServiceProvider();
var seedService = sp.GetRequiredService<ISeedService>();
await seedService.SeedAsync();

Key signal: If you're using IServiceProvider in your service layer or business logic, it's wrong. If you're using it in framework/infrastructure code where types are genuinely dynamic, it's acceptable.
followUpAnswers.What is the difference between service l:
Both resolve objects at runtime, but they differ fundamentally in intent, transparency, and testability:

AspectService LocatorFactory Pattern
IntentGeneral-purpose resolution of any serviceCreation of a specific type of object
DependenciesHidden — consumer calls locator for anythingExplicit — factory interface declared in constructor
CouplingCoupled to IServiceProvider (DI container)Coupled only to the factory interface
TestabilityHard — must set up container or mock IServiceProviderEasy — mock the factory interface
DiscoveryCan't tell what it resolves without reading codeFactory interface name reveals what it creates
// Service Locator — problematic
public class PaymentProcessor(IServiceProvider sp)
{
    public void Process(PaymentMethod method)
    {
        var handler = sp.GetService<IPaymentHandler>(); // what does this get?
    }
}

// Factory Pattern — clean alternative
public interface IPaymentHandlerFactory
{
    IPaymentHandler Create(PaymentMethod method);
}

public class PaymentProcessor(IPaymentHandlerFactory factory)
{
    public void Process(PaymentMethod method)
    {
        var handler = factory.Create(method); // intent is clear
    }
}

// Factory implementation uses IServiceProvider internally — that's fine
public class PaymentHandlerFactory(IServiceProvider sp) : IPaymentHandlerFactory
{
    public IPaymentHandler Create(PaymentMethod method) => method switch
    {
        PaymentMethod.Card   => sp.GetRequiredService<CardPaymentHandler>(),
        PaymentMethod.UPI    => sp.GetRequiredService<UpiPaymentHandler>(),
        _ => throw new NotSupportedException()
    };
}

The factory wraps IServiceProvider inside a specific, named abstraction. PaymentProcessor depends on IPaymentHandlerFactory — a clear, testable, mockable interface — not on the container itself.

questions.tip: A great interview signal: say 'I avoid service locator in application code. It's only justified in factory methods or infrastructure-level code where types are determined at runtime.'

questions.question: How do you configure DI in a Worker Service (.NET BackgroundService)? How does it differ from Web API?
questions.answer:
Worker Services use the same .NET Generic Host and DI container as Web APIs, but without ASP.NET Core middleware. The key differences:

  • No HTTP request scope — there are no per-request scopes. Everything is either Singleton or explicitly scoped via IServiceScopeFactory.
  • All services default to Singleton — because BackgroundService is registered as Singleton, its direct dependencies must also be Singleton (or use scope factory).
  • Host lifecycle — IHostedService / BackgroundService starts on StartAsync and runs ExecuteAsync in a background task.

projectExample.explanation: PaymentReminder runs as a Windows Service / Docker container. IEmailService is Singleton because it's stateless and thread-safe. DbContext is Scoped and accessed via IServiceScopeFactory inside ExecuteAsync — creating a new scope per execution cycle to avoid stale EF Core change tracker.

followUps: How do you stop a BackgroundService gracefully with CancellationToken?
What is the difference between IHostedService and BackgroundService?
How do you schedule a BackgroundService to run every N minutes?

followUpAnswers.How do you stop a BackgroundService grac:
The CancellationToken passed to ExecuteAsync is triggered when the host receives a shutdown signal (Ctrl+C, SIGTERM, IHostApplicationLifetime.StopApplication()). Your code must observe this token to stop gracefully.

public class PaymentReminderWorker : BackgroundService
{
    private readonly IServiceScopeFactory _scopeFactory;
    private readonly ILogger<PaymentReminderWorker> _logger;

    protected override async Task ExecuteAsync(CancellationToken stoppingToken)
    {
        _logger.LogInformation("Worker starting");

        // Pass stoppingToken to Task.Delay — cancellation wakes it up immediately
        while (!stoppingToken.IsCancellationRequested)
        {
            try
            {
                using var scope = _scopeFactory.CreateScope();
                var repo = scope.ServiceProvider
                    .GetRequiredService<IReminderRepository>();

                // Pass token to async DB operations too
                await repo.ProcessDueRemindersAsync(stoppingToken);
            }
            catch (OperationCanceledException)
            {
                // Expected — thrown when stoppingToken is triggered during await
                break;
            }
            catch (Exception ex)
            {
                _logger.LogError(ex, "Error in worker cycle");
                // Don't crash the worker — log and continue
            }

            // Waits 5 minutes OR until cancellation, whichever comes first
            await Task.Delay(TimeSpan.FromMinutes(5), stoppingToken);
        }

        _logger.LogInformation("Worker stopped gracefully");
    }
}

Key points:

  • Pass stoppingToken to all await calls — DB queries, HTTP calls, Task.Delay
  • Catch OperationCanceledException separately — it's expected, not an error
  • Configure shutdown timeout in appsettings.json: "ShutdownTimeout": "00:00:30" (30 seconds)
followUpAnswers.What is the difference between IHostedSe:
BackgroundService is an abstract base class that implements IHostedService and simplifies the plumbing for long-running background tasks.

AspectIHostedServiceBackgroundService
TypeInterfaceAbstract class (implements IHostedService)
Methods to implementStartAsync + StopAsyncOnly ExecuteAsync
Background executionManual — you manage the TaskAutomatic — ExecuteAsync runs on a background Task
CancellationTokenManual wiringAutomatically passed to ExecuteAsync
Use whenShort-lived startup/shutdown tasks, precise lifecycle controlLong-running background loops (the common case)
// IHostedService — full control, useful for startup/shutdown work
public class DatabaseMigrator : IHostedService
{
    public async Task StartAsync(CancellationToken ct)
    {
        // Runs at startup, before app accepts requests
        await ApplyMigrationsAsync(ct);
    }
    public Task StopAsync(CancellationToken ct) => Task.CompletedTask;
}

// BackgroundService — for long-running background loops
public class InvoiceProcessor : BackgroundService
{
    protected override async Task ExecuteAsync(CancellationToken stoppingToken)
    {
        while (!stoppingToken.IsCancellationRequested)
        {
            await ProcessBatchAsync(stoppingToken);
            await Task.Delay(TimeSpan.FromMinutes(1), stoppingToken);
        }
    }
}

For PaymentReminder-style periodic tasks, always extend BackgroundService — it's less boilerplate and handles the background thread automatically.
followUpAnswers.How do you schedule a BackgroundService:
The built-in approach uses Task.Delay inside the ExecuteAsync loop. For production scheduling, consider PeriodicTimer (.NET 6+) or Quartz.NET for CRON-style scheduling.

Option 1 — Task.Delay (simple, works for basic scenarios):

protected override async Task ExecuteAsync(CancellationToken ct)
{
    while (!ct.IsCancellationRequested)
    {
        await DoWorkAsync(ct);
        await Task.Delay(TimeSpan.FromMinutes(5), ct); // 5-minute interval
    }
}

Option 2 — PeriodicTimer (.NET 6+, preferred):

protected override async Task ExecuteAsync(CancellationToken ct)
{
    // Advantage over Task.Delay: tick fires on schedule even if previous work was slow
    // Task.Delay(5min) after 3min work = 8min total cycle
    // PeriodicTimer(5min) = ticks every 5min regardless of work duration
    using var timer = new PeriodicTimer(TimeSpan.FromMinutes(5));

    while (await timer.WaitForNextTickAsync(ct))
    {
        await DoWorkAsync(ct);
    }
}

Option 3 — Quartz.NET (for complex scheduling, CRON expressions):

// Install: dotnet add package Quartz.Extensions.Hosting
builder.Services.AddQuartz(q =>
{
    var jobKey = new JobKey("InvoiceJob");
    q.AddJob<InvoiceProcessingJob>(opts => opts.WithIdentity(jobKey));
    q.AddTrigger(opts => opts
        .ForJob(jobKey)
        .WithCronSchedule("0 0 9 * * ?")  // every day at 9am
    );
});
builder.Services.AddQuartzHostedService(q => q.WaitForJobsToComplete = true);

Rule of thumb: Use PeriodicTimer for simple fixed intervals. Use Quartz.NET for complex scheduling (CRON, specific times, missed-fire handling).

questions.tip: Real interview signal: know that Worker Services don't have HTTP request scope and explain IServiceScopeFactory. Most developers know Web API DI but fewer know the Worker Service nuances.

questions.question: What are extension methods for IServiceCollection and why do you create them?
questions.answer:
Extension methods on IServiceCollection let you group related service registrations into named methods, keeping Program.cs clean and making the registration intent explicit.

Instead of registering 15 services directly in Program.cs, you create builder.Services.AddApplicationServices(), AddInfrastructure(), etc. This follows the SRP principle — each extension method owns one area of registration.

projectExample.explanation: FolioTrack's Program.cs had all registrations inline. Extracting to extension methods makes it readable: you can see at a glance what 'application services' vs 'infrastructure' means, and adding new services doesn't bloat Program.cs.

followUps: How does this relate to Clean Architecture layer separation?
What are the assembly scanning patterns for auto-registration?

followUpAnswers.How does this relate to Clean Architectu:
In Clean Architecture (and its variants like Onion Architecture and Vertical Slice Architecture), layers are separated into distinct projects/assemblies. Each layer owns its own DI registrations via extension methods — preventing Program.cs from knowing about implementation details of each layer.

Typical Clean Architecture DI wiring:

// Solution structure:
// MyApp.Domain      — entities, interfaces (no DI references)
// MyApp.Application — use cases, command/query handlers
// MyApp.Infrastructure — EF Core, HTTP clients, email providers
// MyApp.API         — controllers, Program.cs

// MyApp.Application/ServiceCollectionExtensions.cs
public static class ApplicationServiceExtensions
{
    public static IServiceCollection AddApplication(this IServiceCollection services)
    {
        services.AddMediatR(cfg =>
            cfg.RegisterServicesFromAssembly(typeof(ApplicationServiceExtensions).Assembly));
        services.AddAutoMapper(typeof(ApplicationServiceExtensions));
        services.AddValidatorsFromAssembly(typeof(ApplicationServiceExtensions).Assembly);
        return services;
    }
}

// MyApp.Infrastructure/ServiceCollectionExtensions.cs
public static class InfrastructureServiceExtensions
{
    public static IServiceCollection AddInfrastructure(
        this IServiceCollection services, IConfiguration config)
    {
        services.AddDbContext<AppDbContext>(o =>
            o.UseSqlServer(config.GetConnectionString("Default")));
        services.AddScoped<IInvoiceRepository, EfInvoiceRepository>();
        services.AddScoped<IEmailService, SmtpEmailService>();
        return services;
    }
}

// MyApp.API/Program.cs — clean, each project owns its registrations
builder.Services.AddApplication();
builder.Services.AddInfrastructure(builder.Configuration);

The key benefit: The API project (Program.cs) doesn't need to know that Infrastructure uses EF Core or SMTP. If you swap EF Core for Dapper, only InfrastructureServiceExtensions changes — Program.cs doesn't change at all.
followUpAnswers.What are the assembly scanning patterns:
Assembly scanning auto-discovers and registers services by convention, eliminating the need to manually register each class. Two approaches: Scrutor (library) and manual reflection.

Pattern 1 — Marker interface convention:

// All services implement a marker interface indicating their lifetime
public interface ITransientService { }
public interface IScopedService { }
public interface ISingletonService { }

public class EmailService : IEmailService, IScopedService { }
public class CacheService : ICacheService, ISingletonService { }

// Auto-register by scanning for marker interfaces
var assembly = typeof(Program).Assembly;

foreach (var type in assembly.GetTypes().Where(t => !t.IsAbstract))
{
    if (typeof(IScopedService).IsAssignableFrom(type))
        foreach (var iface in type.GetInterfaces()
            .Where(i => i != typeof(IScopedService)))
            services.AddScoped(iface, type);

    if (typeof(ISingletonService).IsAssignableFrom(type))
        foreach (var iface in type.GetInterfaces()
            .Where(i => i != typeof(ISingletonService)))
            services.AddSingleton(iface, type);
}

Pattern 2 — Scrutor (cleaner, recommended):

// Install: dotnet add package Scrutor
services.Scan(scan => scan
    .FromAssemblyOf<IApplicationMarker>()
    .AddClasses(c => c.AssignableTo<IScopedService>())
    .AsImplementedInterfaces()
    .WithScopedLifetime());

// Or by namespace
services.Scan(scan => scan
    .FromAssemblyOf<Program>()
    .AddClasses(c => c.InNamespaces("MyApp.Services"))
    .AsImplementedInterfaces()
    .WithScopedLifetime());

When to use: Large projects (20+ services) where manual registration becomes a maintenance burden. The tradeoff is less explicitness — you can't see all registrations in one place, which can make debugging DI issues harder. Always add exclusion mechanisms for special cases.

questions.tip: Extension methods for IServiceCollection are a standard clean architecture pattern. Mention it shows you think about maintainability, not just making the code work.

questions.question: What is Scrutor? How does assembly scanning work for automatic DI registration?
questions.answer:
Scrutor is a NuGet package that extends IServiceCollection with assembly scanning capabilities. Instead of manually registering each service, you can auto-register all classes that implement a specific interface or match a convention.

It uses a fluent API with services.Scan() to find and register services by convention. The built-in .NET container doesn't support scanning natively — Scrutor adds this capability cleanly without replacing the container.

projectExample.explanation: In large projects with 20+ services, manual registration becomes maintenance burden. Scrutor auto-discovers by convention. Adding a new AuthService that implements IAuthService registers it automatically — no Program.cs change needed.

followUps: What are the risks of automatic assembly scanning?
How do you exclude specific classes from auto-registration?

followUpAnswers.What are the risks of automatic assembly:
Assembly scanning trades explicit control for convenience. The risks are real in production codebases:

1. Accidental registration: Any class matching the scanning criteria gets registered — including test helpers, internal utilities, or partially-implemented classes you didn't intend to register. If a developer adds a class that coincidentally implements a scanned interface, it silently becomes a registered service.

2. Wrong lifetime assignment: Scanning assigns the same lifetime to all discovered classes. A class that should be Singleton (because it holds expensive shared state) gets registered as Scoped if the scan rule says Scoped. The bug is silent — no exception, just wasted initialization cost or thread-safety issues.

3. Debugging difficulty: When a dependency resolution fails, you can't quickly grep Program.cs to see what's registered. You have to understand the scanning rules and mentally simulate what they would discover.

4. Breaking changes: Moving a service class to a different namespace or renaming an interface can silently un-register it from the scan. The app compiles fine, but throws at runtime when the service is first requested.

5. Duplicate registrations: If you scan multiple assemblies and a service exists in more than one, you may get duplicate registrations — the last one wins, potentially overriding an intentional registration.

// Risk: this scans EVERYTHING in the assembly
services.Scan(scan => scan
    .FromAssemblyOf<Program>()
    .AddClasses()              // too broad!
    .AsImplementedInterfaces()
    .WithScopedLifetime());

// Safer: narrow the scan scope
services.Scan(scan => scan
    .FromAssemblyOf<Program>()
    .AddClasses(c => c
        .InNamespaces("MyApp.Services")    // specific namespace
        .Where(t => t.Name.EndsWith("Service")))  // naming convention
    .AsImplementedInterfaces()
    .WithScopedLifetime());
followUpAnswers.How do you exclude specific classes from:
Scrutor provides multiple exclusion mechanisms — from predicate filters to custom attributes:

Option 1 — Where predicate (exclude by type/name):

services.Scan(scan => scan
    .FromAssemblyOf<Program>()
    .AddClasses(classes => classes
        .InNamespaces("MyApp.Services")
        .Where(type =>
            !type.Name.StartsWith("Abstract") &&   // exclude abstract bases
            !type.IsNested &&                        // exclude nested classes
            type != typeof(SpecialManualService)))  // exclude specific type
    .AsImplementedInterfaces()
    .WithScopedLifetime());

Option 2 — Custom exclusion attribute (most explicit):

// Define a marker attribute
[AttributeUsage(AttributeTargets.Class)]
public class ExcludeFromScanAttribute : Attribute { }

// Mark classes to exclude
[ExcludeFromScan]
public class InternalHelperService : IHelperService
{
    // manually registered elsewhere with specific config
}

// Filter in scan
services.Scan(scan => scan
    .FromAssemblyOf<Program>()
    .AddClasses(classes => classes
        .InNamespaces("MyApp.Services")
        .WithoutAttribute<ExcludeFromScanAttribute>())
    .AsImplementedInterfaces()
    .WithScopedLifetime());

// Then register manually with specific lifetime
services.AddSingleton<IHelperService, InternalHelperService>();

Option 3 — Mixed approach (scan most, register exceptions manually):

// 1. Scan with broad rules
services.Scan(scan => scan
    .FromAssemblyOf<Program>()
    .AddClasses(c => c.InNamespaces("MyApp.Services"))
    .AsImplementedInterfaces()
    .WithScopedLifetime());

// 2. Override specific registrations AFTER the scan
// Last registration wins for single-service injection
services.AddSingleton<ICacheService, DistributedCacheService>();

Best practice: Define clear, narrow scanning rules (specific namespaces + naming conventions) rather than relying on exclusions. Exclusion lists tend to grow as the codebase grows, eventually negating the value of scanning.

questions.tip: Scrutor is not in every project but knowing it exists shows depth. The interviewer may have used it — bring it up when discussing large-scale DI management.
